Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent Green phase: no change - 7 runes already passing

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll work through the Refactor phase systematically.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43

content.text: Just completed cycle 10 (7 runes → 198). Let me check tests pass and analyze the current state.

content.text:
All 10 tests pass. Now let me analyze the current state.

The orchestrator says "Recent Green phase: no change - 7 runes already passing". This means the 7-runes test (cycle 10) passed without any change because:
- 7 runes: `length === 3` is false, so `isThreeAlikeRunes` is false
- `rawBasePrice` = 7 × 25 = 175
- premium = 175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → ceil = 198 ✓

**Wait** — this is suspicious. The test name says "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base". The expected base is 175. With the current `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60` for 3-rune block, 1 block of 3 = 60, plus 4 individuals × 25 = 100, total = 160. But the test expects 175 (which is just 7 × 25 with no block applied).

Let me re-check: 7 × 25 = 175. The test comment says "1 block of 3 + 4 individuals" yields 175. So 1 block of 3 should be 75 (3×25), plus 4 individuals × 25 = 100, total 175. That makes the block price for 3 runes here = 75, not 60.

But wait — for the 3-runes-only test, it expects 60 base (the block discount). So 3 alone = 60, but 1 block within 7 = 75?

Re-reading test 10: "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base, premium 198 G". 175 = 7 × 25. So the "block" doesn't apply a discount here — only when exactly 3 runes are insured does the 60G block price kick in. The current `isThreeAlikeRunes` (length === 3) correctly captures this.

So the test description is misleading (mentions "1 block of 3 + 4 individuals") but the expected numeric result is consistent with the current narrow `length === 3` guard. The test 9 (4 runes) and test 10 (7 runes) both confirm: the block discount only applies when there are exactly 3 runes.

Now for refactoring evaluation:

## Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

- `quote`: domain action — still fits
- `items`, `rawBasePrice`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`: all clear
- `isThreeAlikeRunes`: describes "all items are runes AND there are exactly 3" — accurate
- `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`: precisely the discounted price for exactly 3 runes
- `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`: all clear

All names still fit accurately.

## APP Mass — Current

```typescript
const items = input.items;
const rawBasePrice = items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0);
const isThreeAlikeRunes = items.length === 3 && items.every((item) => item.type === "rune");
const basePrice = isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice;
const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE;
return Math.ceil(premium);
```

Approximate mass (function body only):
- Bindings: items, rawBasePrice, sum, item, isThreeAlikeRunes, basePrice, firstInsuranceSurcharge, premium, item (every) = ~9
- Constants: 0, 3, "rune" = 3
- Invocations: reduce, [item.type access], length, every, ceil, + ops, * op, === ops = ~13
- Conditionals: ternary, && = 2 (mass 8)
- Loops: reduce, every = 2 (mass 10)
- Total ~ 9 + 3 + 26 + 8 + 10 = ~56

## Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass — Yes (10 passing).

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent

Looking again at the code: `isThreeAlikeRunes` checks `length === 3 && every is rune`. The variable name says "three alike runes" but the check is specifically "three items AND all are runes". The name is reasonably accurate.

Is there an intent improvement? Looking at `rawBasePrice` vs `basePrice` — the distinction is clear (raw = sum-by-type, basePrice = after block-discount applied).

### Rule 3: No Duplication

No obvious duplication. The block-discount logic exists once.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements

Could we inline `rawBasePrice` into the ternary?
```typescript
const basePrice = isThreeAlikeRunes 
  ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE 
  : items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0);
```

This would save one binding (mass −1) but tangle the two concepts. The current separation reads "compute raw base, then apply block override" — natural narration. Per cycle-5 precedent, Rule 2 > APP.

Could we inline `firstInsuranceSurcharge`?
```typescript
const premium = basePrice * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE) + PROCESSING_FEE;
```

Same trade-off as cycle 5 (already rejected): test names enumerate three distinct components ("base + first-insurance + fee").

### Considering Generalization

The 7-runes test (cycle 10) confirmed that "block" applies only at exactly 3 runes. Future tests will introduce:
- "2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block (different types)" — still length === 3 BUT different types
- "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base" — THIS is the forcing test for generalisation: it requires per-type counting and `floor(count/3)` block logic at base 60/block

So generalising NOW (before the moonstone test) would be speculative triangulation. Cycle-8 already documented this exact reasoning: wait for the genuine forcing test.

### Considering Extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a Function

It's used once; inlining preserves locality (cycle 8 precedent).

### Considering Renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock`

The current name describes the structural condition ("three alike runes"); the proposed name describes the consequence ("qualifies for block discount"). The consequence-oriented name better reveals intent — it links the condition directly to the domain rule it triggers. This is a small Rule 2 improvement.

But cycle 8 explicitly rejected renaming this var (kept it accurate as a "narrow guard" for the single data point). Now with 2 negative-case tests (cycles 9 and 10) confirming the condition is intentionally narrow, the name `isThreeAlikeRunes` accurately captures structure but a more domain-oriented name could be marginally better.

However, the upcoming "2 runes + 1 moonstone" and "3 runes + 3 moonstones" tests will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune-counting). Renaming now would just churn — the name will need to change again in 1-2 cycles. Better to leave it.

### Looking for any clear win

Examining one more time:

```typescript
const items = input.items;
```

This binding aliases `input.items` from a single-property argument. With 3 usages of `items` (reduce, length, every), the alias pays off (cycle 9). Keep.

The body is dense but each binding earns its keep. The structure mirrors the domain narration: "given items, compute their raw base price, check for a block discount, apply first-insurance surcharge, add fee, round up the premium."

### Decision

After exhaustive evaluation, no refactoring improves the code right now. The 7-runes test was a confirming test (like cycle 9), not a forcing test. The structure that emerged in cycles 8-9 already absorbs the cycle-10 case. The actual forcing test for block generalisation is cycle 12 (3 runes + 3 moonstones).

I'll document this as a "no refactoring (justified)" cycle, mirroring the cycle-5 and cycle-2 precedents.

- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. input.new_string:
## After cycle 9
- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.

## After cycle 10
- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete — Cycle 10 (7 runes test)

**Naming Evaluation (first priority)**:
- `quote`: domain action — still fits
- `isThreeAlikeRunes`: accurately describes the structural condition; considered renaming to consequence-oriented (`qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock`) but rejected because cycle 12 will reshape the predicate entirely
- All other names (`items`, `rawBasePrice`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, constants) still fit

**Current Code Mass**: ~56 (unchanged from cycle 9)

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): all 10 passing
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): no clear improvement; existing names map directly to test vocabulary
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): none found; cycle 9 already de-duplicated `input.items` accesses
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): considered inlining `rawBasePrice` and `firstInsuranceSurcharge` — rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent (test names enumerate distinct components, Rule 2 > APP)

**Refactoring Applied**: none

**Justification**: Cycle 10 is a second confirming test for the narrow `length === 3` block guard (cycle 9 was the first). The numeric expectation 175 = 7 × 25 (no block discount applied across 7 runes) reinforces the cycle-8 decision to keep the guard narrow. The structural generalisation to `floor(count/3)` blocks would still need restructuring once cycle 12 (3 runes + 3 moonstones) introduces per-type block counting, so generalising now is premature triangulation. Architecture notes appended with cycle-10 reasoning.

**Tests**: 10 passing, 28 todo.
